from_raw: 4-methoxyphenyl 4-nitrophenyl methane (diarylmethane with OMe and NO₂ groups)

to_raw: mesityl carboxylic acid + MeOH — BAL2 hydrolysis
tertiary carbocation (3 degree) via 1,2-hydride or 1,2-methyl shift
1-methyl-1-phenylpropan-2-ylium (CH3)2C+-CH2Ph (3 degree) via 1,2-phenyl shift through phenonium ion

from_raw: exocyclic carbocation on cyclohexane ring system
to_raw: endocyclic carbocation (C+ moves into ring) via 1,2-H shift in ring system

from_raw: cyclobutylcarbinyl cation (cyclobutane ring with exocyclic C+)
to_raw: cyclopentyl cation (C+ inside 5-membered ring) via 1,2-bond shift ring expansion

from_raw: cyclopentylcarbinyl cation (cyclopentane ring with exocyclic C+)
to_raw: cyclohexyl cation (C+ inside 6-membered ring) via 1,2-bond shift ring expansion

from_raw: cycloheptyl carbocation (7-membered ring with exocyclic C+)
to_raw: cyclohexyl carbocation (6-membered ring, endocyclic C+) via 1,2-bond shift ring contraction
methyl radical CH3 dot + chlorine radical Cl dot

from_raw: carboxylic acid derivative (benzoic acid) with ortho substituent G
nitrene R-N: (singlet or triplet, 6e- on N, neutral electrophile)
to_raw: nitrene insertion product or rearrangement product

from_raw: dicarboxylic acid (oxalic, malonic, succinic, glutaric, adipic) — first ionization
to_raw: mono-carboxylate anion (first conjugate base); Ka1 order O > M > S > G > A
dicarboxylate dianion; Ka2 order O > G > A > S > M (malonic LEAST acidic second ionization)
cyclopropylcarbinyl cation (cyclopropyl-CH₂⁺) + X⁻ [fastest ionization with AgNO₃]
R-Nu (substitution product via SN2 with nucleophile)
cyclohexanol via ring-expansion through diazonium and 1,2-C shift

from_raw: R-L (alkyl halide or sulfonate, primary or secondary)
to_raw: Nu-R (substitution product) with INVERSION of configuration via SN2

from_raw: HO-(CH2)n-Cl (omega-chloro alcohol, n=4 for 5-chloro-1-pentanol or n=3 for 4-chloro-1-butanol)
to_raw: Cyclic ether (tetrahydropyran for n=4; tetrahydrofuran for n=3) via intramolecular SN2
R-Nu with NET INVERSION of configuration via 2-step sequence: tosylation then SN2

from_raw: Cyclobutylcarbinyl halide (cyclobutane ring with exocyclic C-X)
to_raw: Cyclopentanol or cyclopentyl ether (ring-expanded product, MAJOR) via SN1 with 1,2-C shift

from_raw: Triarylmethyl carbinyl chloride with mixed aryl groups (e.g., Ph₂C(p-OMe-Ph)-CH₂Cl or triphenyl-type with EDG/EWG aryl)
to_raw: Rearranged tertiary alcohol via SN1 with selective 1,2-aryl shift (p-OMe-Ph migrates preferentially)

from_raw: Chiral alcohol R-OH (primary or secondary, optically pure)
to_raw: Alkyl chloride R-Cl with RETENTION of configuration via SNi (thionyl dichloride, no base)

from_raw: Chiral alcohol R-OH (primary or secondary, optically pure)
to_raw: Alkyl chloride R-Cl with INVERSION of configuration (thionyl dichloride + amine base)

from_raw: ω-Hydroxyaldehyde (open-chain form: HO-(CH₂)n-CHO, n=3 or 4) in equilibrium
to_raw: Cyclic hemiacetal (lactol: tetrahydrofuranyl-OH for n=3, tetrahydropyranyl-OH for n=4) via ring-chain tautomerism
(1-Bromopropyl)benzene (Ph-CHBr-CH₂CH₃, benzylic bromide) via SN1 with two sequential 1,2-hydride shifts [JEE Mains Jan 2024]

from_raw: Spiro compound with OH at spiro junction (spiro[4.4]nonan-1-ol or similar, with unequal ring sizes e.g., 4+5 ring spiro)
to_raw: Bicyclic bromide (bridged or fused bicycle) via SN1 with 1,2-C shift ring expansion at spiro junction
Alkyl halide R-X with INVERSION of configuration via reaction with phosphorus trihalide (PX₃)

from_raw: tert-Butyl isopropyl ether ((CH₃)₃C-O-CH(CH₃)₂) — ether with 3° and 2° alkyl groups
to_raw: 2-Bromo-2-methylpropane ((CH₃)₃CBr) + propan-2-ol ((CH₃)₂CHOH) via SN1 at tertiary C

from_raw: Cyclic aliphatic ether (ring containing O; e.g., tetrahydrofuran, tetrahydropyran, oxacycloalkane) treated with HI
to_raw: α,ω-diiodoalkane (or ω-iodoalcohol with 1 equivalent, diiodide with excess HI)

from_raw: 1,3-Benzodioxole (methylenedioxybenzene, the O-CH₂-O bridge on catechol) or 1,4-benzodioxane (O-CH₂CH₂-O bridge on catechol)
to_raw: Catechol (1,2-benzenediol) + diiodoalkane (CH₂I₂ or ICH₂CH₂I) via cleavage of 2 C-O bonds with 2 equiv HI

from_raw: Simple dialkyl ether R-O-R' (both R and R' are simple alkyl groups, no aryl) treated with dilute H2SO4 or H3O+
to_raw: NO REACTION — ethers are completely resistant to hydrolysis under dilute acid conditions

from_raw: Dialkyl ether R-O-R' treated with concentrated H2SO4 at high temperature OR with H3O+ under vigorous conditions (NOT dilute acid)
to_raw: Two alcohols: R-OH + R'-OH (complete ether hydrolysis to both component alcohols)

from_raw: Alkyl halide R-X + dry Ag2O (silver oxide WITHOUT water, anhydrous conditions)
to_raw: Symmetrical ether R-O-R (Williamson-type; both R groups come from R-X; 2 moles R-X per 1 mole Ag2O)

from_raw: Unsymmetrical epoxide (one tertiary C, one primary C adjacent to O) in acidic conditions (H+, HA)
to_raw: Trans-diaxial diol or amino alcohol product with nucleophile (Nu) at the MORE substituted (3 degree) carbon and OH at the LESS substituted (1 degree) carbon

from_raw: Substrate with beta-thioether (R-S-) neighboring group and leaving group on adjacent carbon (alpha-LG compound), in anti (trans) relationship
to_raw: Episulfonium bridged intermediate → product with RETENTION of configuration via double inversion (two enantiomers if symmetric episulfonium)

from_raw: Substrate with beta-OR or beta-OH neighboring group and leaving group, where the OR can form a 5- or 6-membered cyclic intermediate (NOT 3- or 4- or 7-membered)
to_raw: Cyclic oxocarbenium/epoxide/ether bridged intermediate then product with retention of configuration; 4000x rate enhancement when O forms 5 or 6-membered ring NGP

from_raw: Alpha-bromo carboxylic acid (2-bromoalkanoic acid, with COOH and Br on adjacent or same carbon) treated with Ag2O/H2O/NaOH (silver oxide + water + base)
to_raw: Alpha-hydroxy carboxylic acid with RETENTION of configuration (via carboxylate NGP forming cyclic lactone intermediate, then H2O opens with second inversion = overall retention); contrast: conc. NaOH gives INVERSION (simple SN2)

from_raw: Substituted benzene ring at beta position doing pi-NGP (phenonium ion formation) with leaving group; G-substituent on phenyl ring controls rate
to_raw: Phenonium ion (spiro-bicyclic bridged cation where phenyl ring bridges two carbons) + product from external nucleophile opening the phenonium; rate proportional to electron donation by G: -OMe(30x) > -Me(11x) > H(1x) > Cl(0.3x)

from_raw: Substrate with C-C sigma bond bearing EWG substituents (CF3) at the participating carbon, doing sigma-NGP (anchimeric assistance from C-C sigma bond), compared to substrate with H substituents
to_raw: Dramatically reduced sigma-NGP rate when EWG (CF3) replaces H on the participating C-C bond; rate drops from 14 x 10^12 (R1=R2=H) to 1 (R1=R2=CF3) — a 10^13 range

from_raw: Primary allylic halide (e.g., but-2-en-1-yl bromide = CH3-CH=CH-CH2-Br, Br at less hindered primary allylic C) under aqueous conditions with hydroxide (aq KOH)
to_raw: MAJOR: SN1 unrearranged product — OH at original C-Br position, pi bond unchanged (but-2-en-1-ol = CH3-CH=CH-CH2-OH); MINOR: SN1' rearranged product — OH at gamma allylic C with migrated pi bond (but-3-en-2-ol = CH3-CH(OH)-CH=CH2)

from_raw: Branched allylic halide (Br at the more hindered secondary or tertiary allylic carbon, Nu = harder-to-attack C-Br) treated with strong nucleophile (KCN, NaOH, etc.) under SN2-type conditions
to_raw: MAJOR: SN2' product — Nu at gamma carbon (less hindered, remote end of allylic system), pi bond migrates; MINOR: SN2 product (direct at C-Br is sterically disfavored due to hindered C-Br)

from_raw: Allylic alcohol (pent-2-en-1-ol type = CH3-CH=CH-CH2-OH) treated with SOCl2 (thionyl chloride)
to_raw: MAJOR: SNi' product — rearranged allylic chloride (Cl at gamma C, pi bond migrated, e.g., CH3-CH(Cl)-CH=CH2 from but-2-en-1-ol) via 6-membered cyclic transition state; MINOR: SNi product — unrearranged allylic chloride (Cl at original C-OH position, retention) via 4-membered cyclic TS

from_raw: Cyclic allylic halide (cyclohex-2-en-1-yl bromide or similar ring allylic Br, less hindered C-Br in ring) treated with nucleophile under SN2 conditions (KCN, NaOH, etc.)
to_raw: MAJOR: direct SN2 at C-Br (less hindered in ring) = SN2 product (unrearranged); MINOR: SN2' product (at gamma C of ring = rearranged); contrast with branched allylic where SN2' may dominate

from_raw: Acid derivative R-C(=O)-L (acyl halide, ester, anhydride, or amide) with nucleophile Nu: attacking the carbonyl carbon (SNth = nucleophilic substitution in acid derivatives)
to_raw: R-C(=O)-Nu (new acid derivative with Nu replacing L) + L- (leaving group expelled); proceeds through TETRAHEDRAL intermediate R-C(-O-)(-L)(-Nu) with two slow steps

from_raw: Ester R-C(=O)-OR treated with thiolate Nu (RS-) compared to ester reacted with alkoxide (RO-); OR comparison of ester (R-C(=O)-OR) vs thioester (R-C(=O)-SR) in SNth reactivity
to_raw: Thioester (R-C(=O)-SR) undergoes SNth FASTER than ester (R-C(=O)-OR) because SR- is a better leaving group than OR- (thiols are more acidic than alcohols, making thiolate a weaker base = better LG)

from_raw: Acyl halide series (R-C(=O)-F, R-C(=O)-Cl, R-C(=O)-Br, R-C(=O)-I) in SNth reactions — comparing rates across the halide series
to_raw: Rate order: R-C(=O)-I > R-C(=O)-Br > R-C(=O)-Cl > R-C(=O)-F (based on leaving group ability of halide ions in the LG departure step of SNth)

from_raw: Benzene ring with EWG (-M group like NO2) at a specific position AND two chlorines (Ca at ortho/para to EWG, Cb at meta to EWG) treated with methoxide (CH3O- Na+, 1 equiv) — SNAr selectivity
to_raw: Selective substitution at Ca (the Cl ortho or para to EWG) by OCH3; Cb (Cl meta to EWG) remains unreacted; mechanism: Meisenheimer complex stabilized by EWG at ortho/para positions to the substituting Cl

from_raw: Substrate with BOTH an acyl leaving group (C=O-L, SNth site) AND an sp3 carbon with leaving group (sp3-C-LG, SN2 site) in the same molecule, treated with limited nucleophile (1 equiv NaOH, 1 equiv Nu-)
to_raw: SNth product only (acyl C reacts, sp3 C-LG unreacted); the acyl C=O group is substituted by Nu PREFERENTIALLY over the sp3 C; this is the SNth > SN2 selectivity principle

from_raw: Secondary or tertiary alcohol (R-OH) treated with tosyl chloride (TsCl = 4-methylbenzenesulfonyl chloride = para-CH3-C6H4-SO2-Cl) in pyridine or base
to_raw: Tosylate ester R-O-Ts (R-O-SO2-C6H4-CH3-para) with RETENTION of configuration at the C bearing OH; mechanism: SNth at sulfonyl S (not SN2 at C), so C-O bond NOT broken = no inversion at C

from_raw: 2,4-Dinitrochlorobenzene or 2,4-dinitrobromobenzene (Ar-L with NO2 at both ortho and para to L) treated with weak or strong nucleophile under mild conditions
to_raw: SNAr product (Nu replaces L) proceeds readily even with weak nucleophiles (water, alcohols) because two NO2 groups at BOTH ortho AND para positions provide maximal Meisenheimer stabilization; rate increases additively with each EWG at ortho/para

from_raw: 1-Bromo-3,5-dinitrobenzene (Br at C1, NO2 at C3 and C5 = BOTH at META to Br) treated with nucleophile (NaSMe, NaOH, etc.)
to_raw: No reaction (X) — SNAr does NOT occur because both EWG (NO2) groups are at META positions to the leaving group Br; meta-EWG cannot stabilize the Meisenheimer complex at C1; the SNAr condition requires -M group at ORTHO or PARA to L

from_raw: Aryl chloride, aryl bromide, aryl iodide comparison in SNAr: which halide leaves fastest in SNAr when the ring is activated by EWG at ortho/para to the halide
to_raw: In SNAr (addition-elimination), the LEAVING GROUP ABILITY order (I > Br > Cl > F) does NOT control selectivity; instead the rate is controlled by the +ve charge intensity at ipso. When two different halogens on a ring (or two similar substrates) are compared, F makes the ipso C most electrophilic = reacts fastest in SNAr, NOT because F is a better LG but because F makes the ring most activated for the rate-determining addition step

from_raw: Asymmetric aryl halide (substituted chlorobenzene, ring has EDG or -I groups only, H at two positions adjacent to Cl) treated with NaNH2 in liquid NH3 — benzyne mechanism giving three products
to_raw: Three products A + B + C in ratio 1:2:1; B (the central isomer) is DOUBLE because two independent benzyne pathways both give B; this 1:2:1 ratio is the diagnostic signature of the benzyne mechanism with a ring symmetric about the C-Cl bond

from_raw: Aryl chloride with methoxy group (-OCH3) at C4 (para to Cl at C1) treated with KNH2 or NaNH2 in liquid NH3 via benzyne mechanism
to_raw: CINE SUBSTITUTION = MAJOR product (NH2 at C2, adjacent to C1 where Cl was) because OMe +M resonance enriches C1 (para to OMe) making C1 less electrophilic; NORMAL substitution = MINOR (NH2 at C1 same as Cl); cine substitution is the unexpected major product

from_raw: Aryl chloride with methyl group (CH3, +I inductive EDG) at C4 (para to Cl at C1) treated with NaNH2/NH3(l) via benzyne mechanism — regioselectivity
to_raw: CINE SUBSTITUTION = MAJOR (weak preference: NH2 at C2 adjacent to C1) because CH3 +I inductive effect slightly enriches C1 (para to CH3) making C1 marginally less electrophilic; NORMAL = MINOR; the effect is WEAKER than OMe (+M) because +I << +M

from_raw: Alcohol (secondary or tertiary, R-CHOH-R' or R2C(OH)R'') treated with concentrated acid or solid oxide catalyst — dehydration (1,2-elimination of water) to form alkene
to_raw: Alkene (>C=C<) via 1,2-elimination of H2O (H from beta-C, OH from alpha-C); reagents: conc. H2SO4/Delta (most common), conc. H3PO4/Delta, conc. POCl3/Delta, conc. Al2O3/Delta (alumina, solid acid), or conc. ThO2/Delta (thorium dioxide, solid oxide); all require heat (Delta)

from_raw: Alkyl halide (R-CHbeta-CalphaX) treated with strong base via E2 mechanism — concerted anti-periplanar bimolecular elimination
to_raw: More substituted alkene (Saytzeff/Zaitsev major product) via concerted E2: base removes beta-H simultaneously with departure of alpha-LG; transition state is anti-periplanar (dihedral H-Cbeta-Calpha-L = 180 degrees); 2nd order kinetics; rate = k[R-L][Base]; rate order: 3 degrees > 2 degrees > 1 degree; LG order: R-F < R-Cl < R-Br < R-I (slowest to fastest); Delta-H = +ve (endothermic) -> TS looks like product (Hammond); anti-periplanar TS required (syn-periplanar NOT preferred)

from_raw: Rigid bridged bicyclic compound with leaving group (halide) at a ring carbon where ALL adjacent beta-H atoms are locked in gauche (non-anti-periplanar, dihedral NOT 180 degrees) positions relative to the LG — treated with alc. KOH/Delta or any strong base
to_raw: NO REACTION. E2 is geometrically impossible: the anti-periplanar requirement (dihedral H-Cbeta-Calpha-L = 180 degrees) cannot be satisfied because the rigid bicyclic framework locks all beta-H atoms at gauche or syn dihedral angles. No beta-H is anti-periplanar to LG -> no E2 TS can form -> no reaction regardless of base strength or conditions.

from_raw: Cyclohexyl halide (halide at ring carbon) treated with alc. KOH/Delta — E2 product determined by anti-periplanar requirement (not Saytzeff when they conflict); rate depends on LG ability AND axial vs equatorial position
to_raw: Alkene product determined by which beta-H is ANTI-PERIPLANAR (trans-diaxial, dihedral=180 degrees) to the LG in the ring; if anti-periplanar requirement and Saytzeff give different products, ANTI-PERIPLANAR WINS (Anti-elimination > Saytzeff, BOXED rule); rate order on cyclohexyl halides: I-axial > Br-axial > Br-equatorial > Cl (2>1>3>4 in teacher's Q7)

from_raw: E2 elimination substrate comparison: normal beta-H compound (CH2-CH2-Cl, beta-H = protium H) vs deuterium-labeled compound (CD2-CH2-Cl, beta-H = deuterium D) — both treated with alc. KOH/Delta
to_raw: Rate r1 (H-compound) > r2 (D-compound); kH/kD > 1 (Kinetic Isotope Effect, KIE, is observed, BOXED); C-H bond breaking is in the rate-determining TS of E2; this KIE observation is mechanistic EVIDENCE that E2 is concerted (C-H breaking and C-L breaking in the same TS) — if mechanism were E1 (C-L breaks in RDS, C-H breaks in fast step), NO KIE would be observed

from_raw: E2 on acyclic substrate with multiple distinct beta-carbons (e.g. 2-bromobutane or similar secondary/tertiary alkyl halide) — multiple sets of beta-H in different anti-periplanar conformations, treated with alc. KOH/Delta
to_raw: Multiple alkene products (No. of products = 2 or 3 depending on substrate symmetry); each distinct beta-C generates a different alkene; the more substituted alkene (Saytzeff product) is major in acyclic E2; in acyclic systems Saytzeff rule applies normally (unlike cyclic where anti-elimination > Saytzeff)

from_raw: Acyclic secondary alkyl halide where the beta-carbon has TWO CHEMICALLY INEQUIVALENT beta-H atoms (labeled Ha and Hb on the same beta-C) — E2 with alc. KOH/Delta gives stereoisomeric alkenes from the same beta-C
to_raw: Ha and Hb at the same beta-C give DIFFERENT geometric (E/Z) alkene stereoisomers in E2: when Ha is anti-periplanar to LG -> one face -> E alkene; when Hb is anti-periplanar to LG -> other face -> Z alkene; these count as TWO SEPARATE PRODUCTS. Combined with alkenes from other beta-C: No. of products = 3 (OVAL, teacher's Q8). Ha/Hb distinction is the reason for cis+trans products from one beta-C.

from_raw: E2 on rigid substrate where ALL beta-H atoms are gauche (non-anti-periplanar) to LG — e.g. rigid bicyclic or highly substituted cyclohexane with CH3 at multiple ring positions blocking all anti-diaxial positions for the LG
to_raw: NO REACTION (No rxn, underlined). E2 is geometrically impossible: the rigid framework places all beta-H atoms in gauche or syn positions relative to LG (dihedral H-Cbeta-Calpha-L ≠ 180 degrees). No anti-periplanar arrangement possible -> no E2 TS -> no elimination product, regardless of base strength. Q3-alpha from teacher: rigid bicyclic with CH3 at multiple positions + Br + alc. KOH/Delta -> No rxn.

from_raw: Substrate with multiple beta-H environments where one possible E2 alkene product is CONJUGATED (resonance stabilized, e.g. conjugated with adjacent pi system in the molecule) and another is the more substituted Saytzeff alkene (but not conjugated)
to_raw: CONJUGATED/RESONANCE-STABILIZED alkene = MAJOR product; 'Resonance > Sytzeff' (BOXED by teacher); when resonance stabilization of one alkene product conflicts with Saytzeff substitution preference, RESONANCE WINS over Saytzeff. The conjugated (less substituted but resonance-stabilized) alkene is preferred via product-like E2 TS (Hammond: TS resembles more stable product -> conjugated alkene has better resonance delocalization -> more stable -> preferred)

from_raw: Deuterium-labeled substrate with CHD at beta-carbon adjacent to alpha-C-Br — two diastereomers (diastereomer 1: specific relative config; diastereomer 2: inverted relative config) treated with alc. KOH — E2 stereospecificity demonstrated
to_raw: DIASTEREOMER 1 -> TRANS (E) alkene EXCLUSIVELY (anti-periplanar H removed, D stays -> Me and D on same side of C=C = trans/E product, labeled 'Trans' underlined by teacher); DIASTEREOMER 2 -> CIS (Z) alkene EXCLUSIVELY (anti-periplanar H removed from different face, D ends up on same side as Me -> cis/Z product, labeled 'Cis' BOXED by teacher). E2 IS STEREOSPECIFIC: different diastereomers give DIFFERENT alkene stereochemistry. The KIE ensures H is removed faster than D when H is anti-periplanar, but the GEOMETRY (anti-periplanar) determines WHICH atom (H or D) is actually removed from each diastereomer.

from_raw: E2 product counting for complex substrates (polycyclic, bicyclic, or substrates with multiple beta-C types) — general rule application
to_raw: Total No. of products = Total number of distinct alkene structures (RULE stated by teacher: 'Total No. products = Total # alkenes'); count each alkene constitutional isomer separately AND count each E/Z stereoisomer separately; for each beta-C: if the resulting alkene has E/Z isomerism -> count 2; if no E/Z -> count 1; C+T notation (cis + trans) = 2 products from that beta-C; Saytzeff product = major; BOXED: application of all E2 rules to complex multicyclic substrates

from_raw: Cyclohexyl halide with LG (Br or Cl) in EQUATORIAL position in the chair conformation, treated with alc. KOH/Delta or any strong base (E2 conditions)
to_raw: NO REACTION (No rxn). Teacher's NOTE (page 3, RM lec8): 'If leaving group in chair form present at equatorial position, E2 cannot take place bcos there is no periplanar H atom.' Equatorial LG has NO anti-periplanar (180 degree) beta-H in the chair because: the equatorial LG is at ~60 degrees to all adjacent axial H atoms (gauche, not anti-periplanar) and at ~60 degrees to equatorial H atoms. No beta-H can achieve the required 180-degree H-Cbeta-Calpha-L dihedral in the rigid chair conformation -> E2 geometrically impossible -> No reaction regardless of base strength.

from_raw: erythro-2-Bromo-3-deuterobutane (2-bromo-3-deuterobutane, ERYTHRO diastereomer: Br at C2-right and D at C3-right in Fischer projection = Br and D on same side in Fischer) + alc. KOH/Delta
to_raw: CIS (Z)-2-butene-3-d exclusively (labeled '(Cis)' by teacher in Q2 of RM lec8). E2 stereospecificity: anti-periplanar H removal from ERYTHRO substrate gives CIS alkene. Mechanism via Newman: in anti-periplanar conformation of erythro substrate, H(C3) is anti to Br(C2) while D(C3) is gauche to Br(C2) -> E2 removes H -> D remains on C3 of alkene -> the alkene has Me(from C2-CH3) and D(from C3) on SAME SIDE of C=C -> CIS. ERYTHRO -> CIS is the stereospecific outcome.

from_raw: threo-2-Bromo-3-deuterobutane (2-bromo-3-deuterobutane, THREO diastereomer: Br at C2-right and D at C3-LEFT in Fischer projection = Br and D on OPPOSITE sides in Fischer) + alc. KOH/Delta
to_raw: TRANS (E)-2-butene-3-d exclusively (labeled '{trans}' BOXED by teacher in Q3 of RM lec8). E2 stereospecificity: anti-periplanar H removal from THREO substrate gives TRANS alkene. Mechanism via Newman: in anti-periplanar conformation of threo substrate, H(C3) is anti to Br(C2) -> E2 removes H -> D remains -> Me(C2) and D(C3) end up on OPPOSITE sides of C=C -> TRANS. THREO -> TRANS is the stereospecific outcome. BOXED by teacher.

from_raw: Alkyl halide or alkyl substrate with two beta-H types (at different beta-C positions) treated with BULKY BASE (tert-butoxide, LDA, DBN, DBU) in E2 conditions — Hofmann Rule conditions
to_raw: LESS SUBSTITUTED (less crowded) alkene = MAJOR product (Hofmann Rule / Hofmann product). Teacher: 'less crowded alkene will be formed' (underlined). The bulky base cannot easily access the more hindered (more substituted) beta-H due to steric clash between the large base and the alkyl groups at the beta-C; it preferentially removes the LESS HINDERED (primary, terminal) beta-H -> terminal or less substituted alkene = major. Contrast: small base (CH3O-, EtO-, iPrO-, alc. KOH) -> Saytzeff (more substituted alkene). Bulky base (t-BuO-, LDA, DBN, DBU) -> Hofmann (less substituted alkene).

from_raw: Alkyl substrate with two beta-H types and a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG: F, SR2+, NR3+ = sulfonium, ammonium, fluoride) treated with alc. KOH or OH- in E2 conditions — PLG gives Hofmann selectivity
to_raw: HOFMANN alkene (less substituted) = MAJOR product. Teacher's rule (page 9): 'E2 + Poor leaving group -> Follow Hofmann.' PLG rate order: 1 degree > 2 degree > 3 degree (REVERSED from GLG's 3>2>1). Quantitative data from table: SR2+ gives 30% Saytzeff / 70% Hofmann; NR3+ gives 18% Saytzeff / 82% Hofmann; extreme PLG gives 2% Saytzeff / 98% Hofmann. Mechanism: PLG makes TS carbanion-like at beta-C (E1CB-like) -> 1 degree carbanion more stable -> primary beta-H preferred -> Hofmann. BOXED YELLOW: '1 degree > 2 degree > 3 degree' for PLG E2.

from_raw: Quaternary ammonium salt (R-CH2-CR'(NR3+)-...) = alkyl substrate with NR3+ as leaving group treated with OH-/Delta — Hofmann elimination
to_raw: HOFMANN alkene (less substituted = major) + NR3 (tertiary amine = the leaving group departs). NR3+ is a POOR LEAVING GROUP (PLG) in E2 -> Hofmann selectivity: 18% Saytzeff / 82% Hofmann to 2% Saytzeff / 98% Hofmann depending on specific ammonium. Rate order: 1 degree > 2 degree > 3 degree (carbanion TS). The leaving group NR3 (amine) departs as neutral amine. 'Hofmann elimination' = the classic name for E2 of ammonium salts. From teacher's Q (page 10): R-NMe3+ + OH-/Delta -> Hofmann alkene + NMe3.

from_raw: Amine N-oxide (R-CH2-N+(R')2-O- = tertiary amine N-oxide, also called 'ammonium oxide') heated with Delta — Cope elimination (Ei mechanism)
to_raw: Alkene (Hofmann = less substituted, major) + hydroxylamine derivative (R'2NOH). Mechanism: Ei (internal elimination) via a 5-membered cyclic TS: N-oxide O- acts as internal base abstracting SYN beta-H (O-N-Calpha-Cbeta-H ring = 5 atoms); ANTI-PERIPLANAR IS NOT REQUIRED (syn-periplanar instead); H and O are on the SAME SIDE (syn). Rate order: 1 degree > 2 degree > 3 degree (Hofmann selectivity). This is the COPE ELIMINATION. Products: alkene + R2NOH (hydroxylamine). From teacher pages 11-12: N-oxide substrate + Δ -> alkene + N-containing byproduct (shown as N with ring).

from_raw: Any substrate giving two possible regioisomeric alkenes in E2 (two different beta-C positions with H's) — testing whether student knows REGIOSELECTIVE vs REGIOSPECIFIC classification for E2
to_raw: E2 is REGIOSELECTIVE: gives Saytzeff (more substituted) as MAJOR + Hofmann (less substituted) as MINOR — two different regioisomers, one is major. NOT regiospecific (which would require 100% of one regioisomer). Teacher's statement (page 2): 'E2 is a regioselective rxn' (underlined). Contrast with E1 (H+ conditions): alpha = 100%, omega = 0% (via carbocation gives ONLY Saytzeff = approaches regiospecific). The ratio (alpha:omega = major:minor) depends on base size and LG quality (Saytzeff/Hofmann rules).

from_raw: Acyclic alkyl bromide with specific beta-H configurations giving trans/cis geometric isomers in E2 — testing stereoselective classification
to_raw: E2 is STEREOSELECTIVE: trans (E) alkene = MAJOR product, cis (Z) alkene = MINOR product. Trans is major because anti-periplanar transition state leads to trans alkene for acyclic substrates with most common configurations. Teacher's example (page 3): secondary bromide (zig-zag with Br) + alc. KOH -> Trans (labeled 'Trans major') + Cis (labeled 'cis', minor). Teacher's conclusion: 'E2 is a stereoselective rxn' (underlined). Definition: stereoselective = two stereoisomers produced, one is major.

from_raw: Chiral alkyl substrate R-L (with R or S configuration) reacted with nucleophile via SN2, then via SN1 — testing stereospecific vs non-stereospecific classification
to_raw: SN2: STEREOSPECIFIC (R substrate -> S product, 100% inversion; S substrate -> R product, 100% inversion; two different SMs give two different products). SN1: NOT stereospecific. 'Real SN1 is loosely stereoselective (60+40)' (teacher verbatim, page 4) = in practice gives ~60% inversion + 40% retention (not true 50:50 racemic). Reason: departing L- partially shields one face even after ionization (ion pair) -> slightly more inversion than retention. SN1 is 'not stereoselective' (teacher, page 4) in the strict sense but loosely so. SN1 is 'not stereospecific' (teacher, page 6).

from_raw: Beta-phenoxy phenyl sulfone (Ph-SO2-CH(alpha)-CH2-OPh) or beta-hydroxy carbonyl compound treated with OH- or strong base/Delta — E1CB with sulfonyl or carbonyl EWG and phenoxy/alkoxide as leaving group
to_raw: Vinyl sulfone (Ph-SO2-C=CH2) or alpha,beta-unsaturated carbonyl (enone) via E1CB. EWG = Ph-SO2 (sulfonyl = powerful -M group, activates alpha-C-H) or C=O (carbonyl, activates beta-C-H in beta-hydroxy carbonyls). PLG = OPh (phenoxide = relatively stable anion, better than alkoxide but still PLG under basic E1CB conditions) or OH- in beta-hydroxy carbonyl. Teacher's Q6 (page 12): Ph-SO2-CH(alpha)-CH2-OPh + OH-/Delta -> [Ph-SO2-C-(alpha)--CH2-OPh] (carbanion stabilized by SO2Ph) -> Ph-O- departs -> Ph-SO2-C=CH2 (vinyl sulfone, BOXED). Teacher's Q1 (page 11): beta-hydroxy ketone + OH-/Delta -> E1CB -> enone (alpha,beta-unsaturated ketone, dehydration under basic conditions).

from_raw: Ester (R-C(=O)-O-CH2-CHR'-R'') heated to approximately 500 degrees C — Ei pyrolysis of ester (Ei mechanism, no acid or base needed)
to_raw: Alkene (HOFMANN = less substituted = major, from syn elimination) + carboxylic acid (R-COOH). Mechanism: Ei (internal elimination) via 6-membered cyclic TS: the C=O oxygen abstracts the syn beta-H (H on Cbeta that is SYN to OC(=O)R) while Calpha-O bond breaks simultaneously. 6-membered ring TS: O(C=O)...H-Cbeta-Calpha-O-C(=O) = 6 atoms. SYN elimination (H and OAc must be syn-periplanar). Hofmann rule (less substituted alkene = major). Temperature: 500 degrees C (high, no solvent). Products: alkene + R-COOH (carboxylic acid). Teacher: 'Syn elimination' + 'Follow Hofmann rule' (both underlined, page 1). RESONANCE > HOFMANN also applies (Q6 page 2: boxed 'Hoff 7 Reso' = if conjugated alkene possible, that wins). IUPAC: propan-2-yl acetate -> propene + ethanoic acid (example).

from_raw: Alcohol (R-OH) converted to methyl xanthate ester (R-O-C(=S)-S-Me) in 4 steps then heated to approximately 200 degrees C — Chugaev Reaction (Ei pyrolysis of xanthate)
to_raw: Alkene (Hofmann = less substituted = major) + COS (carbonyl sulfide = O=C=S) + MeSH (methanethiol = methyl mercaptan). Mechanism: Ei via 6-membered cyclic TS using S of C=S as internal base abstracting syn beta-H. Products: alkene + O=C=S (COS) + H-S-Me (MeSH). Temperature: approximately 200 degrees C (milder than ester pyrolysis 500 degrees C). PREPARATION: R-OH -> (1) Na -> R-ONa -> (2) CS2 -> R-O-C(=S)-SNa -> (3) Me-I/SN2 -> R-O-C(=S)-SMe (xanthate ester) -> (4) Delta/200 degrees C -> alkene + COS + MeSH. Syn elimination. Hofmann rule. Resonance > Hofmann. IUPAC of xanthate: O-alkyl S-methyl carbonodithioate. Teacher (page 4-5): 'Follow Hofmann rule' + 'It is syn elimination' + 'Hoff < Reso.' (all underlined).

from_raw: Norbornyl (bicyclo[2.2.1]heptyl) ester or similar rigid cage ester substrate heated (Delta) — testing whether Ei/ester pyrolysis occurs in rigid bicyclic systems
to_raw: NO REACTION ('No rxn', underlined by teacher). Teacher Q9 (page 3): norbornyl OAc + Delta -> 'No rxn'. Reason: in the rigid bicyclo[2.2.1]heptane (norbornane) framework, the geometry of the ring system prevents ANY beta-H from achieving a syn-periplanar (0 degree dihedral) arrangement with the OAc leaving group. Ei pyrolysis requires: H and OC(=O)R must be SYN-PERIPLANAR (0 degree dihedral, same side, for the 6-membered cyclic TS to form). In norbornyl, the C-H bonds at beta-C are all ANTI or GAUCHE to the OAc at alpha-C (constrained by the rigid cage) -> no syn-periplanar H available -> no 6-membered TS can form -> no Ei elimination -> no reaction. Compare: if E2 were attempted (anti-periplanar required), norbornyl also resists elimination because anti-periplanar H-C-C-L geometry is also very difficult in the cage. Both anti and syn requirements are problematic in norbornyl. Q10 (page 3): different norbornyl-type OAc (with one accessible syn-H) -> 97.5% one regioisomeric alkene (the specific regioisomer dictated by which H is syn-periplanar).

from_raw: Vicinal diol (1,2-diol = pinnacol = R1-C(OH)(R2)-C(OH)(R3)(R4)) treated with H2SO4 — Pinacol-Pinacolone Rearrangement
to_raw: Pinacolone = ketone (R1-C(=O)-C(R2)(R3)(R4)) via 1,2-R shift (migration of R2 from C bearing OH to adjacent carbocation). Mechanism: (1) H2SO4 protonates one OH -> +OH2; (2) -H2O -> carbocation at alpha-C (resonance: R1-C(OH)(R2)-C+(R3)(R4) <-> R1-C(+OH)(R2)-C(R3)(R4)); (3) R2 MIGRATION (1,2-shift from beta-C to alpha-C+, teacher labels 'R2 shift / Slow'); (4) -H+ -> pinacolone (ketone product, BOXED). Substrate labeled 'Vicdiol / Pinnacol' by teacher. MIGRATION APTITUDE determines WHICH group migrates: Ar > R > H (teacher's rule, page 12b). For aryl migration: p-OMe-Ar >> p-Me-Ar > Ar > p-NO2-Ar (EDG increases aptitude, EWG decreases). Teacher example: substrate with p-NO2-Ph and p-OMe-Ph -> p-OMe-Ph migrates FAST; p-NO2-Ph migrates SLOW.

from_raw: Same secondary amine substrate (R-NMe2, with specific H and D stereochemistry at beta-C) treated via TWO DIFFERENT pathways: (A) MeI/Ag2O/Delta = Hofmann E2 (anti) vs (B) H2O2/Delta = Cope elimination (syn)
to_raw: TWO DIFFERENT alkene products from the SAME substrate depending on mechanism. Pathway A (Hofmann E2: anti-periplanar H required): anti-H removed -> specific E/Z product A. Pathway B (Cope elimination: syn-periplanar H required): syn-H removed -> OPPOSITE E/Z product B. Teacher's Q (page 9): bicyclic NMe2 substrate with H(solid wedge) and D(dashed) -> Branch 1: MeI -> N+Me3; moist Ag2O (gives OH-) -> Hofmann E2 (anti) -> Product with H label. Branch 2: H2O2 -> N-oxide (NMe2+-O-); Delta -> Cope (syn) -> Product with D label = DIFFERENT. This proves: Hofmann E2 (anti-specific) and Cope (syn-specific) are OPPOSITE in stereospecificity. The diastereomers that react in E2 are DIFFERENT from those that react in Cope.

from_raw: Cyclopentyl substrate bearing N-oxide (NMe2+-O-) or xanthate (OCS2Me) with both exo and endo beta-H accessible for syn-periplanar Ei cyclic transition state — testing Hofmann vs Saytzeff in 5-membered ring
to_raw: SAYTZEFF (endocyclic, more substituted) alkene = MAJOR product. NOT Hofmann (exocyclic, less substituted). Teacher pages 1 and 5: cyclopentyl N-oxide 97.5% gives Saytzeff (endocyclic C=C in the ring). Reason: 'In the cyclic 5-membered ring which is nearly planar structure, the formation of endo cyclic double bond (Sytzebb rule) predominates' (teacher's note, page 3). Cyclopentane is nearly planar -> both exo-H (giving exocyclic C=C = Hofmann) and endo-H (giving endocyclic C=C = Saytzeff) can form the Ei cyclic TS, BUT the endo-H TS gives a planar ring TS (all atoms lie in one plane = Good TS) while the exo-H TS is less planar. Teacher also: 'Sytzebb Rule followed & cyclopentane ring hydrogen give preferred planar structure than methyl hydrogen' (page 5, double underlined). CONTRAST: Cyclohexane (chair form) = Hofmann (exocyclic) preferred because axial-equatorial alignment forces exocyclic elimination (page 3: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred'). IUPAC example: methylcyclopentan-1-yl methyl xanthate -> 1-methylcyclopent-1-ene (endocyclic = Saytzeff, MAJOR) + methylenecyclopentane (exocyclic = Hofmann, minor).

from_raw: Cyclohexyl substrate with N-oxide (NMe2+-O-) or xanthate axial at ring carbon, requiring axial-equatorial syn alignment of H and LG — Cope/Chugaev in cyclohexane ring
to_raw: HOFMANN (exocyclic alkene = less substituted, via CH2= exocyclic) = MAJOR product under normal conditions. For cyclohexane: axial-equatorial alignment required for Cope & Chugaev. Teacher note (page 3): 'In the cyclohexane system it requires an axial-equatorial alignment of the eliminating group. (For cope & chugaev only).' IF only ONE syn H is accessible (the axial-equatorial syn H) -> one regioisomeric alkene = major (teacher Q(a): 98% = 'only syn hydrogen'). IF BOTH exo-H AND endo-H are syn (both accessible via axial-equatorial alignment) -> Saytzeff preferred (teacher Q(b): 85%, 'Both are syn / So we preferred Sytzebb t.'). CONTRAST with cyclopentane: cyclohexane (chair) = Hofmann preferred (by default); cyclopentane (planar) = Saytzeff preferred. Teacher: 'in the 6-mem ring which is chair form, the exocyclic double bond (Hoffmann rule) preferred.' (page 3, Note 1)

from_raw: Acyclic diastereomeric substrate with Ph and D labels: erythro and threo isomers of Ph-CH(NMe2+-O-)-CH(D/H)-Ph treated with Delta — Cope elimination stereospecificity (E/Z product ratios)
to_raw: Erythro -> 89% Z (cis-stilbene type) alkene + 11% E. Threo -> 94% E (trans-stilbene type) alkene + 6% Z. Teacher page 6: 'Erythro [arrow] 89% Z' and 'Threo [arrow] 94% E.' Syn Cope elimination is STEREOSPECIFIC (like E2 which is anti-specific but OPPOSITE): erythro -> Z; threo -> E. Not 100% stereospecific for acyclic substrates because conformational flexibility allows minor amounts of the non-syn conformer to react. Rule: syn Cope/Chugaev: erythro -> Z; threo -> E. Compare E2 (anti): erythro -> E; threo -> Z (OPPOSITE assignment). So for the SAME diastereomeric substrate, Cope gives the opposite E/Z product compared to E2. Also: 5-membered Cope TS and 6-membered Chugaev TS from the SAME diastereomeric SM give DIFFERENT E/Z products (teacher Q1 vs Q2, page 2) because the different ring size changes the syn geometry.

from_raw: Cyclohexyl quaternary ammonium (NMe3+) with LG in equatorial position treated with OH- (E2 conditions) — testing E2 vs SN2 when E2 is geometrically blocked
to_raw: SN2 + MeOH (methanol byproduct). NOT E2 (E2 impossible). Teacher Q3 (page 4): 'E2 not possible at eq' (equatorial NMe3+ in cyclohexane). Reason: E2 requires ANTI-PERIPLANAR beta-H (180 degree dihedral H-Cbeta-Calpha-N+). In cyclohexane chair, anti-periplanar requires BOTH the LG (N+) to be AXIAL and the beta-H to be AXIAL on the adjacent C. If LG = equatorial -> there is no anti-axial H available -> E2 cannot form the anti-periplanar TS -> no E2. Alternative pathway: OH- attacks one of the N-methyl groups via SN2 (backside attack on Me-N+ bond) -> MeOH is released (labeled '+ Me-OH' by teacher) + the amine is N-demethylated to NMe2 (now tertiary amine). Teacher also: cyclooctyl NMe3+ + OH- -> bicyclic-type product = 60% (page 4) = likely trans-cyclooctene or bridged product from ring-strain-driven reaction.

from_raw: Amino alcohol H2N-(CH2)n-CH2-Br or similar omega-haloamine undergoing intramolecular SN2 cyclization — Baldwin's rules for ring formation (Fun Fact from teacher, page 3)
to_raw: Cyclic amine (aziridine for n=1, azetidine for n=2, pyrrolidine for n=3, piperidine for n=4, azepane for n=5) via intramolecular SN2. Rate of cyclization (teacher Fun Fact, page 3, yellow): 6-membered ring (piperidine, n=4) = FASTEST (relative rate = 100); 5-membered (pyrrolidine, n=3) = second fastest; 3-membered (aziridine, n=1) = rate 7x; 7-membered (azepane, n=5) = rate 2; 4-membered (azetidine, n=2) = SLOWEST (rate = 1, reference). RATE ORDER: 6-membered (100) > 5-membered >> 3-membered (7x) > 7-membered (2) > 4-membered (1). Teacher label: 'Rate of intramolecular cyclisation = (decreasing arrow).' The 4-membered ring is slowest because of VERY HIGH RING STRAIN in the TS (Baeyer strain in 4-membered cyclobutane-like TS). The 6-membered ring is fastest because: perfect geometry for SN2 backside attack + no ring strain in the 6-membered TS.
higher acidity (lower pKa) via negative inductive effect

from_raw: alkyl group attached to carbon chain
to_raw: +I effect magnitude ordered: tert-butyl > isopropyl > n-propyl > ethyl > methyl > H
-I effect magnitude ordered: fluoro > chloro > bromo > iodo

from_raw: multiple EWG groups at different positions relative to acidic proton
to_raw: acidity ranking by DNP Rule: Distance > Number > Power
-I effect ordered: sp (-C≡N, -C≡C-) > sp2 (-CH=CH2, phenyl) > sp3 (-CH2R) [relative to H]
two acyloxy radicals (2 R-C(=O)-O•) via homolytic O-O fission

from_raw: conjugated π-system with adjacent lone pair (p-π resonance donor)
to_raw: delocalised resonance structure with lone pair donated into π-system
resonance hybrid — actual molecule with intermediate bond lengths and fractional charges
NO resonance stabilisation; each double bond acts independently

from_raw: hydroxybenzene (phenol) — O lone pair adjacent to benzene ring
to_raw: 5 canonical (resonating) structures via +M donation of O lone pair into ring

from_raw: aminobenzene (aniline) — N lone pair adjacent to benzene ring
to_raw: 5 canonical structures via +M donation; electron density increased at ortho and para positions

from_raw: phenylmethanal (benzaldehyde, PhCHO) — polar C=O adjacent to ring
to_raw: 6 canonical structures via -M withdrawal; electron density decreased at ortho and para

from_raw: nitrobenzene (PhNO₂) — polar N⁺=O with N⊕ adjacent to ring
to_raw: 5 canonical structures for ring + 2 internal NO₂ resonance structures

from_raw: +M donor group attached to benzene ring
to_raw: ring activation ordered by +M power: O⁻ > NH₂ > NHR > NR₂ > OH > OR > NHC(=O)R > OC(=O)R > Ph > F > Cl > Br > I
ring electron density ranked: aminobenzene > hydroxybenzene > acetanilide > fluorobenzene > benzene > nitrobenzene > benzonitrile

from_raw: benzonitrile (Ph-C≡N) — cyano group attached to benzene
to_raw: 5 canonical structures via -M withdrawal of C≡N: ⁺ at ortho-2, ortho-6, para-4 with ⁻ on N of CN

from_raw: benzenesulfonic acid (Ph-SO₃H) — sulfonyl group attached to benzene
to_raw: -M effect: S⁺ withdraws π-electrons from ring; multiple canonical structures with ⁺ on ring carbons

from_raw: set of canonical (resonating) structures of a molecule
to_raw: most stable canonical structure identified by Rule I: complete octet is highest priority

from_raw: two canonical structures with complete octets
to_raw: more stable canonical identified by Rules II-IV: π-bonds > charge number > charge position > charge distance

from_raw: canonical structure with positive charge on oxygen/nitrogen/fluorine with incomplete octet
to_raw: FORBIDDEN — this canonical structure cannot exist

from_raw: phenylammonium (aminobenzene protonated, Ph-NH3+) substituent on benzene
to_raw: only 2 canonical structures (Kekulé pair); no +M effect; ring strongly deactivated

from_raw: phenylborane (Ph-BH2) — boron with empty p-orbital attached to benzene
to_raw: 5 canonical structures via -M effect: empty B p-orbital accepts ring π-electrons; ⁻ at ortho and para, B becomes B⁻

from_raw: organic compound (CₓHᵧOᵤ) subjected to complete combustion (Liebig's method)
to_raw: CO₂ absorbed in KOH bulb (mass = m₁ g); H₂O absorbed in anhydrous CaCl₂ U-tube (mass = m₂ g)

from_raw: mass of CO₂ (m₁ g) and H₂O (m₂ g) from Liebig combustion of w₁ g sample
organic compound containing nitrogen (CₓHᵧNᵤ) + CuO → Dumas method
to_raw: CO₂ + H₂O + N₂(g); N₂ collected over KOH solution; volume V₁ measured at P₁, T₁

from_raw: N₂ gas collected at experimental conditions (P₁, V₁, T₁) in Dumas method
to_raw: V₂ (volume at STP) via ideal gas law; then %N = (28/22400) × V₂ × 100/w₁

from_raw: elemental analysis data: %C, %H, %N known from combustion/Dumas
2-amino-3-(4-hydroxyphenyl)propanoic acid (tyrosine) or 2-amino-3-(1H-indol-3-yl)propanoic acid (tryptophan) in protein solution
to_raw: yellow/orange nitro derivatives upon heating with concentrated nitric acid (Xanthoproteic test POSITIVE)
Ruhemann's purple (blue-purple dye) + RCHO (aldehyde) + CO₂ + H₂O

from_raw: alkene (R-CH=CH₂) + dilute sulphuric acid (dil H₂SO₄) or dil HX
to_raw: Markovnikov alcohol: OH at more substituted carbon (R-CH(OH)-CH₃)
anti-Markovnikov alcohol: OH at less substituted carbon (R-CH₂-CH₂-OH)

from_raw: carboxylic acid (R-COOH) — reduction to primary alcohol
to_raw: R-CH₂-OH: achieved ONLY by LAH, BH₃·THF, or H₂/Ru-C. SBH (NaBH₄), LiBH₄, H₂/Pt, H₂/Pd, H₂/Ni all FAIL

from_raw: compound with O-H (alcohol, phenol, carboxylic acid) + Na metal
to_raw: sodium alkoxide/phenoxide/carboxylate + ½H₂ gas (effervescence); acidity hierarchy governs minimum base needed

from_raw: primary or secondary alkan-1-ol/alkan-2-ol + ceric ammonium nitrate reagent [(NH₄)₂Ce(NO₃)₆]
to_raw: RED coloured complex [Ce(NO₃)₄(ROH)₃] initially; after time → colourless (alcohol oxidised to aldehyde/ketone, Ce⁴⁺ → Ce³⁺)

from_raw: primary, secondary, or tertiary alcohol — Victor Meyer test (for degree determination)
to_raw: 1° → RED solution in NaOH (nitrolic acid); 2° → blue liquid insoluble in NaOH (pseudonitrole); 3° → colourless (no reaction with HNO₂)

from_raw: primary nitroalkane (R-CH₂-NO₂, primary α-H) + nitrous acid (HNO₂ / HONO)
to_raw: nitrolic acid [R-C(-NO₂)=N-OH]: dissolves in NaOH to give RED sodium salt (R-C(-NO₂)=N-ONa)

from_raw: alcohol (R-OH) or amine (R-NH₂) + acylating agent (acyl chloride R'COCl, acetic anhydride (CH₃CO)₂O, or ketene CH₂=C=O)
to_raw: acetate ester (R-O-C(=O)-CH₃) from alcohol, or acetamide (R-NH-C(=O)-CH₃) from amine; molecular weight increases by 42 per -OH acetylated

from_raw: alcohol (R-OH) + concentrated sulphuric acid — product depends on temperature
to_raw: T > 170°C → alkene (E1, intramolecular dehydration, Zaitsev); 110°C < T < 170°C → ether (R-O-R, intermolecular, SN1); T < 110°C → alkyl hydrogen sulphate (R-O-SO₂-OH)

from_raw: ethane-1,2-diol (ethylene glycol, HO-CH₂-CH₂-OH) + benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (terephthalic acid, p-HOOC-C₆H₄-COOH) — condensation polymerisation
to_raw: poly(ethylene terephthalate) PET [Terylene] — polyester [-O-CH₂-CH₂-O-C(=O)-C₆H₄-C(=O)-]ₙ + n H₂O

from_raw: pinacol (2,3-dimethylbutane-2,3-diol or vicinal diol Ph₂C(OH)-C(OH)Ph₂) + H⁺ — Pinnacol-Pinacolone rearrangement
to_raw: pinacolone (ketone) via 1,2-aryl or 1,2-alkyl shift; migration aptitude :Ar > R > H (within Ar: p-OMe > p-Me > Ph > p-Cl > p-NO₂; within R: 3° > 2° > 1°)

from_raw: benzil (1,2-diphenylethanedione, Ar-CO-CO-Ar) + NaOH/H₂O — Benzil-Benzilic acid rearrangement
to_raw: benzilic acid salt (2-hydroxy-2,2-diphenylacetate, Ar₂C(OH)-COO⁻Na⁺) via 1,2-aryl shift; rate order for aryl substituent effect: EWG-Ar > unsubstituted-Ph > EDG-Ar (controlled by INITIAL ATTACK, not migration); Ph > Ph(Cl) > Ph(OMe)
